topic: Paper 1: Exploring Modern and Literary Heritage Texts
subject: english-literature

items.q: What is the difference between a theme and a symbol?
items.correct: A theme is an abstract idea or concern the text explores (e.g. mortality); a symbol is a concrete image that stands for something else (e.g. a skull).
items.misconception: Themes are just symbols repeated through the book.
items.random_wrong: A theme is the title of the book.
items.why: Students treat theme and symbol as the same thing. Themes are ideas, symbols are images. Skulls (symbol) can develop the theme (mortality), but they are not the same level of abstraction.

items.correct: Experimentation with form (fragmentation, stream of consciousness), focus on subjective interior experience, scepticism toward traditional narrative and absolute truth.
items.misconception: Modernism just means literature written in modern times.
items.random_wrong: Modernism is literature about modern technology like phones.
items.why: Modernism is a specific early 20th-century movement (Joyce, Woolf, Eliot), not a synonym for contemporary. Students use it as a vague chronological term, missing its formal experimentation.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.
The answer examiners want is: Brecht. “Beckett” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who wrote A Streetcar Named Desire?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Williams. “Miller” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Waiting for Godot first performed?
items.why: The real figure is 1953 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
